subject: CS-AI/ML
description: Initial project proposal to address limitations in current continual learning benchmarks. The core idea is to develop a new benchmark suite that better reflects real-world data stream characteristics, such as non-stationarity and concept drift.
reason: This event marks the project's inception. It formalizes vague ideas into a set of concrete research goals, which are then immediately used as a basis for creating the first version of the research plan, establishing the foundational direction for the project.

subject: CS-AI/ML
description: Based on the initial proposal, the team designs the first version of the benchmark scheme. This involves selecting base datasets, defining procedures for generating non-stationary data streams, and specifying the evaluation protocol and metrics for the new benchmark.
reason: With the high-level research goals and plan established in the initial proposal (e_01), the logical next step is to design a concrete experimental methodology. This event translates the abstract goals into a specific, implementable `method_scheme` that will guide the subsequent pilot experiments.

subject: CS-AI/ML
description: The team runs a small-scale pilot study to validate the benchmark generation process and evaluation protocol. A baseline continual learning algorithm (e.g., ER) is tested on a short, generated data stream to ensure the experimental pipeline is working correctly and the chosen metrics are sensitive enough to capture performance changes.
reason: After designing the initial benchmark scheme (e_02), it is crucial to conduct pilot experiments to validate its feasibility and iron out any implementation bugs. This step sanity-checks the `method_scheme` before committing significant computational resources to full-scale main experiments, and it produces the first set of `experiment_results` for preliminary analysis.

subject: CS-AI/ML
description: The team analyzes the results from the pilot study. The analysis confirms the experimental pipeline is working, but it also reveals that the baseline ER algorithm performs unexpectedly well, suggesting the generated tasks might not be challenging enough to differentiate more advanced continual learning methods.
reason: Following the pilot experiments (e_03), a dedicated analysis phase is necessary to interpret the initial findings. By examining the `experiment_results` in the context of the `method_scheme`, the team can validate the setup and identify necessary adjustments before proceeding to larger-scale experiments. This analysis produces an interpreted summary of the results, which will inform the decision to revise the benchmark's difficulty.

subject: CS-AI/ML
description: In response to the pilot analysis showing the tasks were too easy, the team revisits and revises the benchmark design. They decide to increase the task difficulty by introducing more abrupt concept drifts and reducing the data similarity between consecutive tasks in the generated stream.
reason: The analysis in e_04 revealed a critical flaw in the current `method_scheme`—it was not challenging enough. This event is a necessary corrective step to redesign the scheme based on the insights from the pilot `experiment_results`, ensuring the benchmark aligns better with the original `research_goals` before committing to more extensive experiments.

subject: CS-AI/ML
description: A second round of pilot experiments is conducted using the revised, more challenging benchmark scheme from the previous step. The same baseline algorithm (ER) is re-evaluated to verify that the increased task difficulty leads to a more significant performance drop, confirming the benchmark's improved discriminatory power.
reason: After revising the `method_scheme` in e_05 to make it more challenging, it is essential to run another pilot study. This step serves to validate the changes by generating new `experiment_results`, ensuring the benchmark is now sufficiently difficult before the team commits to running full-scale main experiments.

subject: CS-AI/ML
description: The team analyzes the results from the second round of pilot experiments. The findings confirm that the revised benchmark is sufficiently challenging, as the baseline algorithm's performance now degrades significantly. This validates the current method scheme for the main experiments.
reason: After running a second, revised pilot study (e_06), it is critical to analyze the new `experiment_results` to confirm if the design changes from e_05 were successful. This analysis validates that the `method_scheme` now meets the `research_goals` of being sufficiently challenging, which is a prerequisite for moving forward to the large-scale main experiments.

subject: CS-AI/ML
description: With the benchmark design validated, the team launches the full-scale main experiments. This involves running a comprehensive set of state-of-the-art continual learning algorithms on the new benchmark suite, along with several baselines, to collect the primary results for the paper.
reason: The successful validation of the benchmark in the second pilot study (e_07) provides the green light for proceeding with the main, large-scale experiments. This step is essential for gathering the core data required to fulfill the project's research goals and is the logical progression from the iterative design and pilot phase.

subject: CS-AI/ML
description: With the main experiments completed and the methodology finalized, the team begins drafting the paper. They start with the 'Methods' section, detailing the novel benchmark design, the data stream generation process, and the evaluation protocol.
reason: The experimental `method_scheme` was validated in e_07 and used for the main experiments in e_08, making it stable. It is therefore an opportune moment to begin writing the paper by drafting the corresponding methods section, which does not depend on the analysis of the main results. This introduces the `paper_paragraph` artifact for the first time.

subject: CS-AI/ML
description: The team conducts a comprehensive analysis of the main experiment results. This involves plotting performance curves, calculating aggregate metrics, and performing statistical tests to compare the different continual learning algorithms on the new benchmark.
reason: After running the main experiments in e_08, a thorough analysis is the necessary next step to interpret the large volume of `experiment_results`. This analysis will determine the paper's main conclusions by evaluating how well different methods perform in the context of the initial `research_goals`.

subject: CS-AI/ML
description: The team drafts the 'Results' section of the paper based on the comprehensive analysis. This involves creating figures and tables to present the comparative performance of different algorithms on the new benchmark and writing text to summarize the key findings and observations.
reason: Following the comprehensive analysis of the main experiments (e_10), the most logical step is to document these findings. This event translates the analyzed `experiment_results` into the 'Results' section of the paper, building upon the existing draft which already contains the methods section (e_09).

subject: CS-AI/ML
description: While reviewing the main analysis, the team notices an anomaly: all advanced methods show only marginal gains over the simple ER baseline, which contradicts expectations. This triggers a deeper, diagnostic analysis to understand if this is a genuine finding or an artifact of the benchmark design, for example, by looking at layer-wise feature representations or per-task error patterns.
reason: The comprehensive analysis in e_10 yielded surprising results where advanced methods did not outperform a simple baseline. Before writing the paper's conclusions, it is crucial to investigate this anomaly by re-examining the `experiment_results` in the context of the `method_scheme` to ensure the findings are robust and not an unintended artifact, thus properly addressing the `research_goals`.

subject: CS-AI/ML
description: After the diagnostic analysis revealed that advanced methods offer minimal gains, the team pivots the project's focus. They decide the core contribution is not just the benchmark itself, but the finding it reveals: that many SOTA methods fail to significantly outperform strong baselines under realistic concept drift. The research goals are updated to reflect this new, more impactful narrative.
reason: The unexpected results from the main experiments and subsequent analysis (e_12) represent a critical turning point. Instead of trying to force a different result by redesigning the scheme again, the team recognizes the current findings as the most significant outcome. This necessitates a formal revision of the `research_goals` and `research_plan` to frame this surprising discovery as the paper's central thesis, which is a mature and common pivot in a real research project.

subject: CS-AI/ML
description: With the research goals and narrative pivoted, the team rewrites the introduction and drafts the discussion/conclusion sections of the paper. This reframes the work, emphasizing the surprising finding that SOTA methods struggle to outperform a strong baseline on the new benchmark, rather than just presenting the benchmark itself.
reason: After formally pivoting the project's narrative in e_13, the most critical next step is to align the paper with the new `research_goals`. This involves rewriting key sections like the introduction and conclusion to frame the `experiment_results` in the context of the new, more impactful story, building upon the existing paper draft.

subject: CS-AI/ML
description: The team assembles the rewritten introduction, existing methods, results, and new conclusion into a complete first draft of the paper. They also write the abstract and related work sections, ensuring all parts of the manuscript are aligned with the new, pivoted narrative.
reason: After pivoting the narrative (e_13) and rewriting the paper's framing (e_14), the next logical step is to consolidate all written material into a complete manuscript. This involves writing the remaining sections like the abstract and ensuring the entire paper consistently reflects the updated `research_goals` and the story told by the `experiment_results`.

subject: CS-AI/ML
description: The team conducts a thorough internal review of the complete paper draft. They focus on polishing the language, improving clarity, and ensuring a consistent narrative throughout the abstract, introduction, methods, results, and conclusion, making final adjustments to figures and tables.
reason: With a complete first draft assembled in e_15, a dedicated internal review and polishing phase is a crucial next step before submission. This ensures the manuscript is of high quality and effectively communicates the pivoted research goals and the significance of the experimental findings.

subject: CS-AI/ML
description: The team submits the polished manuscript to a top-tier machine learning conference. The project, from initial conception to final paper submission, is now considered complete, pending peer review.
reason: The project has reached a natural conclusion. The research goals have been clarified after a significant pivot (e_13), the experimental methodology is stable, the results are comprehensive and support the revised narrative, and the paper has been fully drafted and internally reviewed (e_16). Submission to a venue is the final step of this research cycle.
